text: The adrenal gland is a small gland that sits just above the kidney in the back of the abdomen. Just like you have two kidneys, you have two adrenal glands.
The purpose of the adrenal gland is to secrete hormones that affect the way your body manages blood pressure, salt and kidney function, as well as stress.

Occasionally, people can develop tumors of the adrenal gland. Although these tumors are usually benign, they can sometimes cause over-secretion of one or more of the hormones produced by the adrenal gland.
When this happens, the treatment for this is to remove the adrenal gland that has the tumor. This is called an adrenalectomy.
This video is of a real adrenalectomy performed at TriHealth. It is being performed minimally invasively using robotic technology.

This video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical adrenalectomy. The purpose of this video is to give patients an idea of how the surgery is actually performed. If you are scheduled to have this procedure, your surgery will be performed in a similar fashion.
The robotic instruments are positioned, revealing the splenic flexure of the colon, which is the segment of the colon nearest to the spleen.
The surgeon identifies the omentum, a layer of fat hanging off the transverse colon, and begins the process of exposing the adrenal gland.
The surgeon navigates the robotic instruments to move surrounding structures aside, revealing the retroperitoneal location of the adrenal gland at the back of the abdomen.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to move the splenic flexure of the colon away, beginning the mobilization of the spleen and distal pancreas.
The surgeon uses their left arm to hold up the spleen, moving it medially to the left side of the screen to further expose the adrenal gland.
The surgeon uses a hook electrocautery instrument to cauterize and divide tissue, hooking behind the tissue to facilitate separation.
The surgeon separates the layers of fat between the inferior border of the pancreas and the colon, visible on the right side of the screen, to further expose the adrenal gland.
The surgeon exposes a portion of the adrenal gland, which appears as a yellowish-orange organ, slightly distinct in color from the surrounding abdominal fat.

The adrenal gland is positioned above the kidney, visible as a bulge to the right of the screen. Blood vessels, including the large renal vein, are observed running from the left to the right of the screen. The surgeon clears the area to improve visibility of these vessels.
The surgeon exposes the adrenal tumor, revealing its larger size compared to a normal adrenal gland due to the benign tumor growth.

The surgeon carefully lifts the pancreas and spleen away from the adrenal tumor, revealing the adrenal gland tucked behind these organs. The gland's location requires precise manipulation of surrounding structures for access.
The surgeon highlights the orange color of the adrenal gland, contrasting it with the surrounding yellow fat tissue and the yellow hue of the pancreas.

The surgeon has fully exposed the adrenal tumor, with the spleen and distal pancreas lifted away from it. The surgical field now focuses on the inferior area to expose the renal vein, which drains the left kidney, and the adrenal vein, which branches off the renal vein to drain the adrenal gland.

The surgeon identifies the small adrenal vein running vertically, contrasting with the larger renal vein running horizontally. The superior border of the renal vein is exposed, with the adrenal gland extending down to the renal vein. The surgeon prepares to divide the adrenal vein while preserving the renal vein, necessitating the removal of tissue above the renal vein for clear visualization.
The surgeon carefully clears surrounding tissue to expose the adrenal vein, preparing to place clips for ligation before dividing the vein.

The surgeon has divided the adrenal vein and is now separating the surrounding tissue from the adrenal gland. The inferior pole of the adrenal gland is visible, held up by the surgeon's left hand, with surrounding fat being carefully removed along with the gland.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer, an energy device that cauterizes and divides tissue, to remove the adrenal gland along with the surrounding yellow fat tissue, ensuring complete excision of the tumor.

The surgeon continues dissection, revealing the kidney at the bottom right of the screen. The kidney appears as a reddish-brown organ, indicating proximity to the adrenal gland and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully removes the surrounding fat from the muscles of the diaphragm and retroperitoneum, ensuring complete clearance of tissue as part of the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to divide the remaining tissue surrounding the adrenal gland, aiming to free the gland completely from its attachments.
The surgeon inserts a retrieval bag through a trocar and places the excised adrenal gland into the pouch, preparing it for removal from the abdominal cavity.
The spleen and pancreas are repositioned, completing the robotic left adrenalectomy procedure.
